summary:
The Cathar Crusade, also known as the Albigensian Crusade (1209–1229 AD), was a 20-year military campaign launched by Pope Innocent III against the Cathar heresy in the Languedoc region of southern France. The Cathars were a dualist Christian sect who believed in two gods — one good and spiritual, one evil and material — rejected the authority of the Catholic Church, its sacraments, and its clergy, and attracted significant popular support throughout Occitania. The assassination of the papal legate Pierre de Castelnau in 1208, blamed on Count Raymond VI of Toulouse who had protected the Cathars, gave Innocent III his pretext: he offered crusader indulgences to northern French nobles who would attack their southern counterparts. The resulting campaign was as much a seizure of the wealthy south by northern nobles as a religious war.

The crusade was marked by extraordinary brutality. At the siege of Béziers in 1209, when a crusader commander was allegedly asked how to tell Catholics from Cathars, the response attributed to the papal legate Arnaud Amaury — 'Kill them all, God will know his own' (Caedite eos, novit enim Dominus qui sunt eius) — encapsulated the campaign's ferocity; the city was massacred and burned. The military leader Simon de Montfort became infamous for his relentless campaigns across the Languedoc, destroying castles, burning Cathar perfecti at the stake, and devastating the region's prosperous culture. The crusade ultimately failed to extirpate Catharism militarily, and the Inquisition — established in the 1230s partly in response — became the instrument of the sect's eventual suppression. The [[event:3786|Albigensian Crusade]] devastated Occitan culture and facilitated French royal absorption of the semi-independent southern counties.
